chunks.title: Course Description
chunks.content: Fundamental concepts and modern methods of artificial intelligence; search (uninformed, informed, iterative improvement, constraint satisfaction, space/time complexity); game playing: minmax, alpha-beta pruning; knowledge representation and reasoning; propositional logic, first-order logic and automated theorem proving, etc.; planning; uncertainty and probabilistic reasoning; machine learning and deep learning basics; selected topics include cognitive models, distributed AI, intelligent agents, perception, robotics, natural language, etc.; philosophical issues, including ethics.

chunks.title: Instructor Details
chunks.content:
Instructor: Yoonsuck Choe
Office: PETR 327
Phone: 979-845-5466 (rarely answered)
E-Mail: choe@tamu.edu
Office Hours: WF 1:00pm-2:30pm via zoom https://tamu.zoom.us/my/yoonsuckchoe

chunks.title: Course Learning Outcomes
chunks.content:
• Understand the basic techniques for creating intelligent programs. This will be measured by homeworks and exams.
• Create successful computer programs illustrating the operation of these methods. This will be measured by the programming assignments.
• Apply the right programming language or technique to the right problem and be able to evaluate a proposed AI application for likelihood of success. This will be measured by programming assignments.

chunks.title: Textbook and/or Resource Materials
chunks.content:
Stuart Russell and Peter Norvig, Artificial Intelligence: A Modern Approach (AIMA, hereafter), 4th Edition, Prentice Hall, New Jersey, 2020.
Book Homepage (Links to an external site.) (you may use 3rd edition)
[Optional] Henry Brighton and Howard Selina, Introducing Artificial Intelligence: A Graphic Guide, Icon Books, 2010.
Book web page

chunks.title: Grading Policy
chunks.content:
• Exams: 55% (midterm: 25%, final: 30%)
• Exam rules
  o All exams will be closed book. Put all books, notes (exception below), cell phones, calculators, or any information containing media in your bag.
  o You may bring a 1-sheet hand-written note (US letter paper: 8.5inch x 11 inch = 215.9 mm x 279.4 mm). You can use both sides. If you made hand-written notes on a tablet (like iPad), you may print it out. Write your full name on the top left corner of page 1. A4 or any other sized paper, two pages glued together, pages ripped off from a notebook, typed (not hand-written) printouts, photocopied or printed notes (except for hand-written tablet notes), name not written or not written in the exact location specified are all in violation of this rule, and will result in the confiscation of your cheat sheet.
  o If you're left handed, sit in a left-handed seat.
  o Bring your student ID, Texas driver's license, or passport. You will not be allowed to take the exam without a photo ID.
  o Aggie honor code will be strictly enforced.
• Homeworks (includes written and programming): 40% (10%, 10%, 10%, 10%).
  o All assignments (including homeworks) are to be done individually. No collaboration or discussion is allowed with any other individual other than the instructor or the TAs.
  o Most programming problems will be accompanied with starter code with a specific data structure. You are required to use the provided data structure and the starter code You are not allowed to code the solution from scratch. Violation will result in 50% penalty on the affected problem.
• Attendance: 5%
• There will be no curving. The cutoff for an 'A' will be >= 89.5% of total score, >= 79.5% for a 'B', >=69.5% for a 'C', >=59.5% for a 'D', and below 60% for an 'F'.

chunks.title: Make up exams and Attendance rules
chunks.content:
Make up exams:
  o Only cases allowed under "Excused Absences" under TAMU Student Rules, Rule 7. AttendanceLinks to an external site. will be eligible for make up exams. See 7.2 Absences and 7.3 Absence Documentation and Verification. Please read this very carefully. (For example, non-acute medical service does not constitute an excused absence, and it is the student's responsibility to provide documentation substantiating the reason for absence.)
  o There will be no make up exam for those who do not show up for the exam without prior notice.
  o Make up exams will be different from the original exams although the difficulty will be adjusted to be comparable.

Attendance rules:
  o Attendance will be taken in class (online form). You are required to attend in-person. The lectures will be recorded using zoom, but you are not allowed to attend the lecture via zoom.
  o Up to 5 absences will be excused. any further absences will result in 1% (out of 5%) per absence.
  o Any attempt of falsified attendance will result in 0% for the attendance grade.
  o Excused absences follow the university rules: Student Rule 7. Example: if you took an excused absence for a family emergency, and were absent from 5 more classes, you will still get full attendance credit.

chunks.title: Late Work Policy
chunks.content: Late penalty: 1 point (out of 100) per hour. Late submissions will not be accepted 4 days after the deadline and/or after the solution has been posted.

chunks.title: AI Tool Usage Policy
chunks.content:
Use of AI tools like ChatGPT is allowed only when it is explicitly stated in the homework description.
You are not allowed to use any AI tools to solve your homework problems (both writing and coding), unless explicitly allowed by the instructor.
